result.summary: Flagged one blocking issue on PR #1171: persistCacheEntry swallows the upsert error and still returns ok, leaving the declared PersistError variant unreachable.

reviewMutations.body: Scope looks right and checks are green; 1 finding in a thread on `persistCacheEntry.ts`.

comments.body: This returns `ok(undefined)` after `saved.isErr()`, so the declared `PersistError` variant is unreachable and every caller reads a failed cache write as a success. Make the signature match the behaviour — either propagate (`if (saved.isErr()) return err(saved.error)`), or keep it best-effort but log the error and change the return type to `Promise<void>` so it stops advertising a failure mode it never returns. An empty `isErr()` branch also means a permanently failing upsert is invisible in production. Add a unit test that stubs `repo.upsert` to the error branch and asserts the chosen contract; that path has no coverage today.

learningMutations.title: Swallowed Result errors: review the contract, not the swallow
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** When a function handles the `isErr()` branch and still returns `ok(...)`, raise the unreachable error variant as the defect and require either propagating the error or narrowing the return type to drop `Result`; do not argue that best-effort behaviour is itself wrong.
**When to apply:** Reviewing any function declared `Result<T, E>` (or Either) whose sad branch falls through to a success return, typically justified by a `// best-effort` comment.
**Anti-pattern:** Demanding error propagation outright — best-effort writes are often intended, so the author dismisses the thread and the lying signature survives. Framing it as a signature/behaviour mismatch holds under either intent, and pairing it with a log request keeps the failure observable.
**Example:** `persistCacheEntry` returned `Promise<Result<void, PersistError>>` but discarded `repo.upsert`'s error and returned `ok(undefined)`, making `PersistError` unconstructable.

result.summary: Reviewed PR #1171; raised one inline finding that persistCacheEntry swallows an upsert failure and returns ok(undefined) despite promising Result<void, PersistError>.

reviewMutations.body: Scope is right and checks are green. One finding, in a thread on `persistCacheEntry.ts`.

comments.body:
`persistCacheEntry` returns `ok(undefined)` even when `repo.upsert` fails, so the declared `Result<void, PersistError>` error path is dead and callers are told a failed write succeeded. Pick one: propagate the failure (`if (saved.isErr()) return err(saved.error)`, or just `return saved`) and let the caller decide whether to ignore it; or, if this really is fire-and-forget, change the return type to `Promise<void>` so no caller can branch on an error that never arrives. Propagation is the better default here — someone declared `PersistError` deliberately.

Whichever you take, add a unit test covering the upsert-failure branch; nothing exercises it today. The `// swallow: best-effort cache write` comment should go with the fix rather than stand in for it.

learningMutations.title: A Result-returning function that never returns err is a signature/behavior mismatch
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** When a function declares `Result<T, E>` but every path returns `ok`, flag it — either propagate the underlying error, or narrow the return type to the plain value so no caller can branch on an error that never arrives.

**When to apply:** Reviewing any function whose body discards a `Result` from a downstream call (repo, gateway, client) and returns success anyway, especially when justified as "best-effort".

**Anti-pattern:** The typed error path stays declared but dead; callers write `isErr()` handling that never fires, and real write failures become invisible in production with no test covering the branch.

**Example:** `const saved = await repo.upsert(entry); if (saved.isErr()) { /* swallow */ } return ok(undefined);` in a function typed `Promise<Result<void, PersistError>>`.

result.summary: Reviewed PR #1171; raised one inline finding on persistCacheEntry swallowing the upsert error and returning ok.

reviewMutations.body: Scope of the change looks right and checks are green. One finding raised in an inline thread.

comments.body: An `upsert` failure is discarded here and the function still returns `ok(undefined)`, so callers cannot distinguish a persisted entry from a dropped one and `PersistError` is unreachable — the typed error channel is dead. Either propagate it (`return saved` in the err branch) and let the caller decide, or, if best-effort really is the contract, drop the `Result` and return `Promise<void>` while logging the failure so it stays observable. Whichever you pick, add a unit test that drives `repo.upsert` into the err branch and asserts the chosen behaviour; nothing exercises that path today.

learningMutations.title: A swallowed err branch under a Result signature is a type error, not a logging gap
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** When a function declares `Result<T, E>` but discards the sad branch and returns `ok(...)`, require either propagation of the error or a signature change to a type that cannot fail — do not accept a log line as the fix.
**When to apply:** Reviewing any Result-returning function whose err branch is empty, commented `best-effort`, or handled only by logging.
**Anti-pattern:** The declared error type becomes unreachable, callers get success on failure, and the dead error path silently outlives the intent that created it.
**Example:** `persistCacheEntry` returned `Result<void, PersistError>` but swallowed `repo.upsert` failures and always returned `ok(undefined)`.

result.summary: Reviewed the attempts pagination change; scope is right, raised one correctness finding (missing ORDER BY) in a single inline thread.

reviewMutations.body: Scope of the change looks right. One correctness finding raised in a thread on the paginated query.

comments.body: `listAttempts` now applies `LIMIT ? OFFSET ?` but the query has no `ORDER BY`, so SQLite's row order is unspecified and can differ between calls — pages can repeat or skip attempts. Add a deterministic ordering to the query, e.g. `ORDER BY created_at, id` (the `id` tiebreaker matters because `created_at` values can collide). The existing unit test only asserts the first page's length; add one that walks two pages and asserts the union has no duplicates and no gaps.

learningMutations.title: LIMIT/OFFSET pagination needs a deterministic ORDER BY
learningMutations.content:
**Rule:** When a query gains `LIMIT`/`OFFSET`, require a total, deterministic `ORDER BY` on the same query — a timestamp column plus a unique tiebreaker such as `id`.
**When to apply:** Reviewing any diff that adds pagination parameters to an existing SQL query, especially SQLite/Postgres `SELECT`s that previously returned a full result set.
**Anti-pattern:** Without an explicit ordering the engine's row order is unspecified, so consecutive pages can repeat or skip rows; unit tests that assert only page length still pass, so the bug ships silently.
**Example:** `SELECT * FROM attempts WHERE task_id = ? LIMIT ? OFFSET ?` needs `ORDER BY created_at, id` before the `LIMIT`.

result.summary: Reviewed PR #231 (paginate attempts list); left one inline finding on the missing ORDER BY in the paginated query.

reviewMutations.body: Scope of the change looks right and checks are green. 1 finding in an inline thread.

comments.body: This query has no `ORDER BY`, so `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` page over SQLite's unspecified row order — rows can be repeated or skipped across pages once the table changes between calls. Add a deterministic ordering: `SELECT * FROM attempts WHERE task_id = ? ORDER BY created_at, id LIMIT ? OFFSET ?`. The existing unit test only asserts the first page's length; add one that pages through a multi-page result and asserts the concatenation covers every row exactly once.

result.summary: Reviewed PR #231; left one inline thread on the paginated attempts query missing a deterministic ORDER BY.

reviewMutations.body: Scope of the change looks right and checks are green. One correctness finding raised in an inline thread.

comments.body: Add a deterministic `ORDER BY` to this query — e.g. `... WHERE task_id = ? ORDER BY created_at, id LIMIT ? OFFSET ?`. SQLite does not guarantee row order without one, so `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` can return the same row on two pages or skip a row entirely as the plan or table state changes. The existing unit test only asserts the first page's length, so it cannot catch this; extend it to page through a fixture set and assert the concatenated ids equal the full ordered set with no duplicates.
